# Transcrição: 10-DCO for Graduation Testing

- **Fonte:** 10-DCO for Graduation Testing
- **Duração:** 04:21
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Now, I want to touch on using dynamic content optimization for the testing phase. So we do have one campaign here, which we did run using DCO. And this was to give an example to my team and also for reference here on how you can use DCO for testing and why generally I prefer not to. So this is phase two of our grad testing. We've got our ad sets that are well performing and within each ad set is one dynamic content optimized ad. So the DCO has different angles built into it and different craters and different variables within that. And you can play around with those different approaches. So for this particular one, we've got three different audiences each with the same DCO. And within the DCO, we have multiple variables. So in the breakdown report, you can have a look at the text, the headline, description, etc. So, you know, we've got multiple, I believe, videos running. So we can see the two different video performances here. Now, here's the problem. This DCO ad has had $134 spent on it in this time period a couple of weeks. And one of the videos has not had enough spend on the actual creative. So the problem with this is with DCO, you can't control the spend unless you remove a variable or a variant from your DCO. You can't actually use, at least right now, use our kind of impressions based rules to pause out ads. So, for example, if this ad had enough impressions, it would naturally get paused out. So if I look at the impressions here, it said 10,000 impressions. So what that means is this ad through the testing phase, this video has got the most impressions and then potentially there are some different headlines and text that have also then got lots of different copy as well. But the problem here is that some of the text won't get any traffic, some of it gets too much, some of it gets okay traffic. And the problem here is, unless you're going in and just removing all the bad performers, which feels like a bit too much work, DCO will struggle to give you a fair test. So I am a fan of using DCO, but not for testing. And this is the reason why that I can't control the outcomes and therefore see where to optimize that spend as well. So like, as you can see, certain videos and headlines have got majority of the spend, because the way DCO works is it's an optimization tool. It's not there for testing. And what I mean by that is DCO is very similar to CBO, Campaign Budget Optimization,

## [03:03 - 04:21]

in that you're letting the computer, the machine, do the work for you. The idea behind a DCO is drop your assets in, your videos, your headlines, your copy creative. And DCO will pick the best variants for that audience. Now, one way of looking at this is, you know, if I look at my report, then, you know, I know that a particular headline and a video and a copy is being shown more than others. So therefore, what Facebook is telling me, and it's doing its job properly, is this particular variation is what Facebook thinks is best for this customer. But it doesn't answer my question as to which ad variables are working and which are not. There's a slight difference there. So for that reason, I prefer, for phase one and for phase two, to use different ad copies, ad create, ad formats, different ads completely, so that can have more granular support over this kind of structure. But also, I can build rules and automations in. So for example, when an ad hits a certain number of impressions, I can pause it out. Whereas with DCO right now, you can't. So that's really the main reason why, at least for testing, I don't like to use DCO.
